novel_features.name: Local publication corpus
novel_features.description: Archive a whole Substack publication into a local SQLite mirror you can read, search, and query offline — no other Substack tool builds a persistent corpus.
novel_features.rationale: Every existing tool fetches live per call; only a local mirror enables offline search, SQL, cross-post analytics, and archive-once/query-forever.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this to turn a live newsletter into a durable, queryable knowledge base instead of re-fetching every time.

novel_features.name: Entitlement-aware read
novel_features.description: Read a post's full text; free posts keyless, and paid posts you subscribe to via your own session cookie — with an honest 'preview only, you're not entitled' signal.
novel_features.rationale: Combines the keyless free path and the user's own Tier-1 credential behind one command with a reliable full-vs-preview detector (audience + body presence).
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use to pull a specific post's full text into an agent workflow, respecting exactly what the user is entitled to.

novel_features.name: Cross-publication digest
novel_features.description: A time-windowed digest across every publication in your local corpus — what's new since you last synced, ranked, in one view.
novel_features.rationale: Requires a local multi-publication store; no single Substack endpoint aggregates across publications.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use as a personal 'what did I miss across my newsletters' briefing.

novel_features.name: Author comparison
novel_features.description: Compare two publications' cadence, topics, and free/paid mix from the local corpus.
novel_features.rationale: A local join across two archived publications that no single API call provides.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use to size up a newsletter before subscribing, or to study what a successful author publishes.

novel_features.name: Local corpus SQL
novel_features.description: Run read-only SQL over your local Substack corpus for arbitrary analytics — post cadence, audience mix, longest posts — from data you've already archived.
novel_features.rationale: The local SQLite mirror allows offline aggregation and joins across archived posts that no single Substack endpoint exposes; a read-only SQL command surfaces that on the CLI (the MCP layer already exposes an equivalent typed tool).
novel_features.example: substack-reader-pp-cli sql "SELECT json_extract(data,'$.audience') AS audience, COUNT(*) FROM resources WHERE resource_type='posts' GROUP BY audience"
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this for ad-hoc analytics over what you've archived, without re-fetching or writing code.

narrative.headline: Read any Substack publication as a local, full-text-searchable corpus — keyless for free posts, your own session for what you subscribe to.
narrative.value_prop: Substack Reader archives whole publications into a local SQLite mirror you can search, SQL-query, and read offline. Free posts need no login; paid posts you're entitled to unlock with your own session cookie — never redistributed, always opt-in. Unlike every other Substack tool it builds a corpus that compounds instead of fetching live per call.
narrative.auth_narrative: Free/public posts are keyless — zero setup. To read paid posts you already subscribe to, provide your own Substack session cookie (substack.sid); this reads only what you are already entitled to and is never required for free content.

quickstart.comment: confirm reachability and config before anything else
mirror a publication's recent posts into the local corpus
read a single post's full text

troubleshoots.symptom: A paid post reads as preview-only despite being subscribed
troubleshoots.fix: Check that your substack.sid session cookie is set (SUBSTACK_SESSION or the config cookie file) and current — an expired or mistyped cookie silently falls back to the public preview. The reader unlocks paid posts through Substack's authenticated by-id endpoint, so a valid session is all that is needed; you never target a particular host yourself.
You are being rate-limited; slow down (archive is self-throttled) — Substack sets no rate headers, so pace requests.

narrative.when_to_use: Use Substack Reader when you want a durable, searchable local copy of one or more Substack publications for reading, agent workflows, or analysis — especially reading a specific post's full text or searching across newsletters offline. It is the right tool when you value a corpus that compounds over live per-call fetching.
anti_triggers: Do not use it to publish, schedule, or manage a Substack you own (this is read-only) — use the Substack web app or a publishing tool.
Do not use it to bulk-scrape or redistribute paid content you are not entitled to — it reads only your own entitled content, on demand.
Do not use it to manage subscribers, payments, or analytics for your own publication.

recipes.title: Build a searchable corpus
recipes.command: substack-reader-pp-cli archive astralcodexten --limit 200 && substack-reader-pp-cli search "prediction markets"
recipes.explanation: Mirror a publication then search it offline with FTS ranking.

recipes.title: Narrow a large post to fields
recipes.explanation: Pull only the fields an agent needs from a verbose post object.

recipes.title: Audience mix analytics
recipes.command: substack-reader-pp-cli sql "SELECT json_extract(data,'$.audience') AS audience, COUNT(*) FROM resources WHERE resource_type='posts' GROUP BY audience"
recipes.explanation: Read-only SQL over the local corpus for arbitrary analytics.
